vulnerability.title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
vulnerability.description: Configure the operating system to elevate the SELinux context when an administrator calls the sudo command. Edit a file in the /etc/sudoers.d directory with the following command: sudo visudo -f /etc/sudoers.d/CUSTOM_FILE Use the following example to build the CUSTOM_FILE in the /etc/sudoers.d directory to allow any administrator belonging to a designated sudoers admin group to elevate their SELinux context with the use of the sudo command: %wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command

triage.reason: While this is a standard sudoers configuration change, modifying SELinux contexts via sudoers carries a risk of locking administrators out of their intended roles if the specified TYPE or ROLE is incorrect or not present in the current SELinux policy. Incorrect context elevation can lead to permission denied errors for critical administrative tasks.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect SELinux type/role specification can break administrative workflows; Potential for administrative lockout from specific system roles

vulnerability.title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
vulnerability.description: The SELinux state should be set to at system boot time. In the file /etc/selinux/config, add or correct the following line to configure the system to boot into enforcing mode: SELINUX= Ensure that all files have correct SELinux labels by running: fixfiles onboot Then reboot the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_selinux_state='' if [ -e "/etc/selinux/config" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^SELINUX=/Id" "/etc/selinux/config" else touch "/etc/selinux/config" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/selinux/config" cp "/etc/selinux/config" "/etc/selinux/config.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "SELINUX=$var_selinux_state" >> "/etc/selinux/config" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/selinux/config.bak" fixfiles onboot else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: While changing the configuration file is low risk, transitioning SELinux from Permissive to Enforcing can cause immediate service failures or system instability if existing files or processes are not correctly labeled. The remediation includes 'fixfiles onboot', which requires a reboot and a potentially long filesystem scan.
triage.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Service disruption due to incorrect SELinux labeling; Potential for system boot failure if critical files are mislabeled; Long downtime during 'fixfiles' execution and reboot

vulnerability.title: Uninstall vsftpd Package
vulnerability.description: The vsftpd package can be removed with the following command: $ sudo dnf remove vsftpd
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # CAUTION: This remediation script will remove vsftpd # from the system, and may remove any packages # that depend on vsftpd. Execute this # remediation AFTER testing on a non-production # system! if rpm -q --quiet "vsftpd" ; then dnf remove -y --noautoremove "vsftpd" fi

triage.reason: While removing an unused service is generally safe, the remediation script explicitly warns that removing the package may trigger the removal of dependent packages. A human must verify that no critical business applications or dependencies rely on vsftpd before execution.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential removal of unintended package dependencies; Disruption of existing FTP services if they are actually in use

vulnerability.title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
vulnerability.description: Modify the /etc/postfix/main.cf file to restrict client connections to the local network with the following command: $ sudo postconf -e 'smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject'
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && rpm --quiet -q postfix; then if ! grep -q ^smtpd_client_restrictions /etc/postfix/main.cf; then echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf else sed -i "s/^smtpd_client_restrictions.*/smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject/g" /etc/postfix/main.cf fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: While the change is a standard configuration hardening for Postfix, modifying 'smtpd_client_restrictions' can immediately break legitimate mail flow from external or non-local clients if the 'mynetworks' parameter is not correctly configured for the specific environment. This requires verification of the existing network topology to ensure mail delivery is not disrupted.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential disruption of legitimate mail relaying from external sources; Dependency on correct 'mynetworks' definition

vulnerability.title: The Chronyd service is enabled
vulnerability.description: chrony is a daemon which implements the Network Time Protocol (NTP) is designed to synchronize system clocks across a variety of systems and use a source that is highly accurate. More information on chrony can be found at https://chrony-project.org/. Chrony can be configured to be a client and/or a server. To enable Chronyd service, you can run: # systemctl enable chronyd.service This recommendation only applies if chrony is in use on the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q chrony; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'chronyd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'chronyd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'chronyd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling the chronyd service is a standard administrative task to ensure time synchronization. The remediation script is non-destructive, checks for package existence before acting, and simply enables/starts a service. It does not impact authentication, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.

vulnerability.title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
vulnerability.description: Chrony is a daemon which implements the Network Time Protocol (NTP). It is designed to synchronize system clocks across a variety of systems and use a source that is highly accurate. More information on chrony can be found at https://chrony-project.org/. Chrony can be configured to be a client and/or a server. Add or edit server or pool lines to /etc/chrony.conf as appropriate: server <remote-server> Multiple servers may be configured.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q chrony; }; then var_multiple_time_servers='' config_file="/etc/chrony.conf" if ! grep -q '^[[:space:]]*\(server\|pool\)[[:space:]]\+[[:graph:]]\+' "$config_file" ; then if ! grep -q '#[[:space:]]*server' "$config_file" ; then for server in $(echo "$var_multiple_time_servers" | tr ',' '\n') ; do printf '\nserver %s' "$server" >> "$config_file" done else sed -i 's/#[ \t]*server/server/g' "$config_file" fi if [[ -s "$config_file" ]] && [[ -n "$(tail -c 1 -- "$config_file" || true)" ]]; then LC_ALL=C sed -i --follow-symlinks '$a'\\ "$config_file" fi fi else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation script attempts to append or uncomment server/pool lines in /etc/chrony.conf. However, the script uses an empty variable 'var_multiple_time_servers' in its loop, which may result in no actual server being added if the grep check fails. Furthermore, adding an incorrect or unreachable NTP server can cause time synchronization issues, which can impact authentication protocols (like Kerberos) and system logging.
triage.estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect NTP server configuration can lead to clock drift; Clock drift can break time-sensitive authentication mechanisms (Kerberos, TOTP); The provided remediation script contains a logic flaw where the server list variable is empty

vulnerability.title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
vulnerability.description: The maxpoll should be configured to in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) to continuously poll time servers. To configure maxpoll in /etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf (or /etc/chrony.d/) add the following after each server, pool or peer entry: maxpoll to server directives. If using chrony, any pool directives should be configured too.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { ( rpm --quiet -q chrony || rpm --quiet -q ntp ); }; then var_time_service_set_maxpoll='' pof="/usr/sbin/pidof" CONFIG_FILES="/etc/ntp.conf" $pof ntpd || { CHRONY_D_PATH=/etc/chrony.d/ mapfile -t CONFIG_FILES < <(find ${CHRONY_D_PATH}.* -type f -name '*.conf') CONFIG_FILES+=(/etc/chrony.conf) } # get list of ntp files for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Set maxpoll values to var_time_service_set_maxpoll sed -i "s/^\(\(server\|pool\|peer\).*maxpoll\) [0-9,-][0-9]*\(.*\)$/\1 $var_time_service_set_maxpoll \3/" "$config_file" done for config_file in "${CONFIG_FILES[@]}" ; do # Add maxpoll to server, pool or peer entries without maxpoll grep "^\(server\|pool\|peer\)" "$config_file" | grep -v maxpo

triage.reason: The remediation involves modifying configuration files (/etc/ntp.conf or /etc/chrony.conf) to add a maxpoll parameter to existing time server entries. This is a standard hardening practice for time synchronization and does not impact system access, networking connectivity, or filesystem integrity.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential for syntax errors if the sed command fails to match patterns correctly

2.  Restarted `chronyd` service to apply the configuration.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan is functionally correct and addresses the vulnerability by ensuring maxpoll parameters are present in the relevant time service configuration files. While the exact value for 'maxpoll' is not defined in the vulnerability description, the approach of inspecting first and then applying the parameter is sound.
concerns: The specific 'maxpoll' value is not provided in the vulnerability description, which could lead to setting an arbitrary value if not clarified.
Automated 'sed' replacement can be brittle if configuration files have unusual formatting or multiple parameters on a single line.
suggested_improvements: Explicitly define the target 'maxpoll' value (e.g., 6 or 4, depending on the specific compliance standard) before execution.
Perform a syntax check of the configuration files (e.g., `chronyd -n` or `ntpd -q`) after modification to ensure no syntax errors were introduced.
Create backups of the configuration files before applying `sed` commands.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan targets the correct configuration files for time synchronization services (chrony/ntp) and uses standard methods (sed) to apply the security hardening parameter. Adjusting maxpoll intervals is a standard configuration change for time services and does not impact system availability, authentication, or core networking.
side_effects: Potential for minor misconfiguration if the sed regex encounters highly non-standard file formatting
Requirement to restart chronyd or ntpd for changes to take effect

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation for the finding **"Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive"** (Rule ID: `xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_chronyd_server_directive`) is complete.

**Remediation Steps Taken:**
1.  **Inspection:** Read `/etc/chrony.conf` and identified the line `pool 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org iburst maxpoll 6`.
2.  **Modification:** Used `sed -i` to replace the `pool` directive with the `server` directive in `/etc/chrony.conf` to comply with the security policy.
3.  **Verification:** Confirmed the update by reading the file, which now contains `server 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org iburst maxpoll 6`.
4.  **Application:** Restarted the `chronyd` service using `systemctl restart chronyd` to apply the changes.

**Final Status:**
The Chrony configuration is now compliant, using the `server` directive for time synchronization.

llm_verdict.message:
The remediation for the finding **"Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive"** (Rule ID: `xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_chronyd_server_directive`) is complete.

**Remediation Steps Taken:**
1.  **Inspection:** Read `/etc/chrony.conf` and identified the line `pool 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org iburst maxpoll 6`.
2.  **Modification:** Used `sed -i` to replace the `pool` directive with the `server` directive in `/etc/chrony.conf` to comply with the security policy.
3.  **Verification:** Confirmed the update by reading the file, which now contains `server 2.rocky.pool.ntp.org iburst maxpoll 6`.
4.  **Application:** Restarted the `chronyd` service using `systemctl restart chronyd` to apply the changes.

**Final Status:**
The Chrony configuration is now compliant, using the `server` directive for time synchronization.

review_verdict.feedback: The plan correctly identifies the core objective: replacing 'pool' directives with 'server' directives to satisfy the hardening requirement. Replacing 'pool' with 'server' is a standard way to remediate this specific finding, although it changes how Chrony handles multiple time sources from a pool-based approach to individual server approaches.
concerns: Replacing a 'pool' directive with a 'server' directive might reduce the redundancy provided by NTP pools if only one server is selected from what was previously a pool.
The plan mentions checking 'driftfile', which is a valid Chrony configuration directive but is not a time source and should not be replaced by 'server'.
suggested_improvements: Ensure that when replacing a 'pool' directive with a 'server' directive, you are aware that the 'pool' directive allows for multiple IP addresses to be resolved from a single DNS name, whereas 'server' treats the name as a single entity (unless multiple server lines are added).
Explicitly clarify that 'driftfile' and other non-source directives (like 'logdir' or 'rtcsync') must remain untouched.
Verify the configuration syntax with 'chronyd -q' or similar check before restarting the service to prevent service failure due to syntax errors.

qa_result.verdict_reason: The plan addresses the security requirement of ensuring time sources use the 'server' directive rather than 'pool'. While replacing a 'pool' with a single 'server' might slightly reduce redundancy, it is a standard hardening procedure for this specific vulnerability. The user correctly identified that the 'driftfile' directive should not be touched, despite mentioning it in the initial thought process.
side_effects: Potential reduction in time source redundancy if a multi-host pool is replaced by a single server directive

vulnerability.title: Uninstall tftp-server Package
vulnerability.description: The tftp-server package can be removed with the following command: $ sudo dnf remove tftp-server
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # CAUTION: This remediation script will remove tftp-server # from the system, and may remove any packages # that depend on tftp-server. Execute this # remediation AFTER testing on a non-production # system! if rpm -q --quiet "tftp-server" ; then dnf remove -y --noautoremove "tftp-server" fi

triage.reason: While removing an unnecessary service is generally a hardening best practice, the remediation script explicitly warns that removing the package may trigger the removal of dependent packages. A human must verify that no critical services or legacy applications rely on the TFTP server before execution.
triage.estimated_impact: Potential removal of unexpected dependent packages; Disruption of existing TFTP-based services or boot processes (e.g., PXE)

vulnerability.title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
vulnerability.description: SSSD should be configured to verify the certificate of the user or group. To set this up ensure that section like certmap/testing.test/rule_name is setup in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. For example [certmap/testing.test/rule_name] matchrule =<SAN>.*EDIPI@mil maprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin}) domains = testing.test
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Enable Certmap in SSSD

triage.reason: Enabling Certmap in SSSD involves modifying the core authentication configuration (/etc/sssd/sssd.conf) with specific mapping rules. Incorrect syntax or incorrect mapping rules can lead to immediate authentication failure for users or groups, potentially locking out administrators or breaking critical service access.
triage.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect sssd.conf syntax can prevent SSSD from starting; Incorrect certmap rules can cause authentication denial for valid users; Changes impact identity and access management (IAM) functionality

vulnerability.title: Enable auditd Service
vulnerability.description: The auditd service is an essential userspace component of the Linux Auditing System, as it is responsible for writing audit records to disk. The auditd service can be enabled with the following command: $ sudo systemctl enable auditd.service
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q audit; }; then SYSTEMCTL_EXEC='/usr/bin/systemctl' "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" unmask 'auditd.service' if [[ $("$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" is-system-running) != "offline" ]]; then "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" start 'auditd.service' fi "$SYSTEMCTL_EXEC" enable 'auditd.service' else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard security hardening practice that does not impact system availability, network connectivity, or user access. The remediation involves unmasking and starting a standard system service.

vulnerability.title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Determine where the audit logs are stored with the following command: $ sudo grep -iw log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf log_file = /var/log/audit/audit.log Configure the audit log to be protected from unauthorized read access by setting the correct permissive mode with the following command: $ sudo chmod 0600 audit_log_file By default, audit_log_file is "/var/log/audit/audit.log".
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if LC_ALL=C grep -iw ^log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then FILE=$(awk -F "=" '/^log_file/ {print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf | tr -d ' ') else FILE="/var/log/audit/audit.log" fi chmod 0600 $FILE else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage.reason: The remediation involves changing the file permissions of the audit log file to 0600. This is a standard hardening practice to prevent unauthorized users from reading sensitive audit data and does not impact system availability or connectivity.
triage.estimated_impact: filesystems affected
